summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

If Q1 = 20, Q3 = 40, what is the upper fence for outliers?

IQR = Q3 - Q1 = 40 - 20 = 20. Upper fence = Q3 + 1.5(IQR) = 40 + 1.5(20) = 40 + 30 = 70.

What is an outlier?

An outlier is an extreme value that lies far from the majority of the data. It can occur on either the high or low end of the distribution.

What is the '1.5 x IQR rule'?

The 1.5 x IQR rule establishes fences at Q1 - 1.5(IQR) and Q3 + 1.5(IQR). Data points beyond these fences are formally classified as outliers.

What is a 'fence' in outlier detection?

Fences are the boundary values calculated using the 1.5 x IQR rule: lower fence = Q1 - 1.5(IQR) and upper fence = Q3 + 1.5(IQR).

What is the 'effect of an outlier on the range'?

Since the range is max minus min, any outlier at the high or low end directly changes the range, often making it much larger than it would be without the outlier.

What is a 'deviation from the mean'?

A deviation from the mean is calculated as (data value - mean). Outliers have very large deviations, which inflate measures like variance and standard deviation.

A real estate agent has home prices: $180K, $190K, $200K, $195K, $185K, $1.2M. What is the range with and without the $1.2M outlier?

With the outlier: $1,200K - $180K = $1,020K. Without: $200K - $180K = $20K. The single outlier multiplied the range by over 50 times.

summary_sections.title: Calculation Methods
summary_sections.content_html:
How to Calculate

Example: For the data {2, 3, 4, 5, 100}, the mean is 22.8 and the median is 4. Which better represents the typical value?

Solution: The value 100 is an outlier that inflates the mean to 22.8, far above most values. The median of 4 is much closer to the other four values and is more representative.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

An outlier is an observation that lies an abnormal distance from other values in the data set, either extremely high or extremely low.

The 1.5 x IQR rule defines outlier boundaries (fences) at Q1 - 1.5(IQR) and Q3 + 1.5(IQR). Any data point beyond these fences is classified as an outlier.

A high outlier pulls the mean upward because it is included in the sum, but the median barely changes because it only depends on the middle position.

The range depends directly on the minimum and maximum values, so an outlier at either extreme dramatically changes the range. The IQR is resistant to outliers.

Outliers pull the mean away from the center. Removing an outlier allows the mean to move back toward the median, making the two measures more similar.

Standard deviation is based on squared deviations from the mean. An outlier creates a very large squared deviation, inflating the standard deviation.

A low outlier decreases the sum of the data, pulling the mean downward, away from the center of the bulk of the data.

IQR = Q3 - Q1 = 40 - 20 = 20. Upper fence = Q3 + 1.5(IQR) = 40 + 1.5(20) = 40 + 30 = 70.

Outliers can result from mistakes (typos, instrument errors) or from genuinely extreme observations that are part of the natural variation in the data.

An outlier is an extreme value that lies far from the majority of the data. It can occur on either the high or low end of the distribution.

A resistant statistic is one that is not heavily influenced by outliers or extreme values. The median and IQR are resistant; the mean and standard deviation are not.

Fences are the boundary values calculated using the 1.5 x IQR rule: lower fence = Q1 - 1.5(IQR) and upper fence = Q3 + 1.5(IQR).

Sensitivity to outliers means the statistic's value shifts substantially when extreme data points are present. The mean and standard deviation are sensitive to outliers.

An outlier's influence refers to how much it changes the value of a summary statistic. A single extreme outlier can have a large influence on the mean and standard deviation.

Since the range is max minus min, any outlier at the high or low end directly changes the range, often making it much larger than it would be without the outlier.

A non-resistant statistic is one that changes significantly when outliers are present. The mean, range, and standard deviation are non-resistant measures.

The sum is 6075, giving a mean of 6075/6 = $1012.50. The median of the sorted data is (215+220)/2 = $217.50. The $5000 outlier makes the mean nearly five times the typical daily revenue.

The 15 is an outlier due to special circumstances. Including it drops the mean from about 88.8 to 78.3, misrepresenting the class. The teacher should note the outlier and consider using the median or reporting both with and without the outlier.

With the outlier: $1,200K - $180K = $1,020K. Without: $200K - $180K = $20K. The single outlier multiplied the range by over 50 times.

Upper fence = Q3 + 1.5(IQR) = 262.5 + 1.5(20) = 262.5 + 30 = 292.5. Since 480 > 292.5, the 480-minute time is formally classified as an outlier.

The 900 ms value creates a very large squared deviation from the mean, inflating the standard deviation. Removing it brings the SD down dramatically because the remaining values are tightly clustered around 255-270.

Without the outlier: mean = 57/7 = 8.1 days. With the outlier: mean = 177/8 = 22.1 days. The single outlier nearly triples the reported average, misrepresenting typical sales.

The range is 9 - 1 = 8, heavily influenced by the outlier. The IQR depends on Q1 and Q3, which are based on the middle 50% of the data and are barely changed by the single extreme value.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A teacher records quiz scores: 88, 90, 85, 92, 87, 91, 15. The 15 was from a student who was absent and took a makeup quiz. Should the teacher include or exclude this outlier when reporting the class average?

The 15 is an outlier due to special circumstances. Including it drops the mean from about 88.8 to 78.3, misrepresenting the class. The teacher should note the outlier and consider using the median or reporting both with and without the outlier.

Example: A real estate agent has home prices: $180K, $190K, $200K, $195K, $185K, $1.2M. What is the range with and without the $1.2M outlier?

With the outlier: $1,200K - $180K = $1,020K. Without: $200K - $180K = $20K. The single outlier multiplied the range by over 50 times.

Example: A basketball player scores the following points in 8 games: 18, 22, 20, 19, 21, 23, 20, 2. The 2-point game was due to an early injury. How do the mean and median change if that game is excluded?

With all 8 games: mean = 145/8 = 18.1, median = 20. Without the 2-point game: mean = 143/7 = 20.4, median = 20. The mean increases significantly while the median is unaffected.

Example: A car dealer tracks days to sell each car: 5, 8, 10, 7, 12, 9, 6, 120. The 120-day car was a rare vintage model. How does this outlier affect the dealer's reported average selling time?

Without the outlier: mean = 57/7 = 8.1 days. With the outlier: mean = 177/8 = 22.1 days. The single outlier nearly triples the reported average, misrepresenting typical sales.

Example: A researcher collects test scores: 70, 72, 74, 76, 78, 80, 82, 84, 86, 88. She accidentally enters 880 instead of 88. How does this error affect the mean?

Correct sum = 790, mean = 79. Erroneous sum = 790 - 88 + 880 = 1582, mean = 158.2. A single data entry error (outlier) changed the mean from 79 to 158.2, more than doubling it.
